essential_question: Can you PROVE, by measuring, that 1/√2 and √2/2 really are the same length?
objective: Match un-rationalized fractions to their rationalized forms in a card sort, and physically measure a unit square's diagonal to confirm that 1/√2 and √2/2 land on the exact same mark.

agenda.title: Brief — From Formula to a Real Length

content.heading: From yesterday's rule to a real ruler
bullets: Yesterday: rationalized denominators on paper by multiplying by √n/√n.
Today: build a real diagonal with a ruler and PROVE two fraction forms measure the exact same length.
The test: does dividing 1 by the measured diagonal land on the same tick mark as half the diagonal's length — BEFORE you trust the algebra?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather graph paper (or a printed 1-inch grid), a ruler, scissors, a calculator, index cards for the sort, and a marker. Decide which square size you'll draw first.

agenda.title: Build — Square It, Measure It, Sort It

content.heading: A length you can hold
bullets: Draw a 1-unit (or 1-inch) square on graph paper and draw its diagonal corner to corner. By the Pythagorean theorem (Lesson 18), 1² + 1² = 2, so that diagonal is exactly √2 units.
Measure the diagonal with your ruler as precisely as you can (it should read close to 1.41). Mark and measure its exact HALF — record both readings.

On a calculator, compute 1 ÷ (your measured diagonal). Compare that decimal to your half-diagonal measurement from the step before — they should be the same number, confirming 1/√2 = √2/2 with a ruler instead of algebra.
Card sort: match each un-rationalized fraction card (like 5/√2, 8/√5, 6/√3) to its rationalized-form card (5√2/2, 8√5/5, 2√3…), naming the radical you'd multiply by for each.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why multiplying by √2/√2 is legal and always clears the denominator, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can construct a unit square's diagonal and use the Pythagorean theorem to identify its exact length as √2
I can measure to confirm that 1/√2 and √2/2 are the same physical length
I can match un-rationalized fractions to their rationalized forms and name the radical used in each

questions.prompt: Draw a 1-unit square on graph paper, draw its diagonal, and use the Pythagorean theorem to show the diagonal's exact length. Record your work.
questions.explain: Rubric: square and diagonal drawn (1 pt) + √2 correctly derived from the Pythagorean theorem (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Measure your diagonal with a ruler, then measure exactly HALF of it. Compute 1 ÷ (your measured diagonal) on a calculator. Record all three numbers and say whether the half-diagonal measurement and the calculator result match.
questions.answer: Example: diagonal ≈ 1.41, half-diagonal ≈ 0.71, 1 ÷ 1.41 ≈ 0.71 — they match, confirming 1/√2 = √2/2.
questions.explain: Rubric: diagonal and half-diagonal both measured and recorded (1 pt) + 1 ÷ diagonal computed and confirmed to match the half-diagonal (1 pt).

questions.prompt: From your card sort, list three fraction/rationalized-form pairs you matched and name the radical you multiplied by for each.
questions.explain: Rubric: three correct pairs (1 pt) + the correct radical named for each (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Reflect: how close did your measured half-diagonal come to the calculator's 1 ÷ √2 value, and what does that closeness tell you about why rationalizing doesn't change a fraction's value?
